Title: Long-term disability insurance — Engineering department
Content:
## Question

How does long-term disability (LTD) insurance work for Engineering employees?

## Answer

LTD provides extended income replacement when an employee cannot work due to a prolonged illness or injury.

### Coverage Details

- **Elimination Period**: 180 days (short-term disability covers the first 26 weeks)
- **Benefit Amount**: 60% of base monthly salary (up to $10,000/month)
- **Duration**: Until age 65, recovery, or return to work (whichever comes first)
- **Cost**: Fully paid by the company (basic LTD); buy-up option available at employee cost

### Eligibility

- All full-time employees enrolled automatically from date of hire
- No health questions for basic coverage
- Buy-up option (70% of salary, up to $15,000/month) requires evidence of insurability

### Definition of Disability

- **First 24 months**: Unable to perform the duties of your own occupation
- **After 24 months**: Unable to perform the duties of any occupation for which you are qualified by education, training, or experience

### Filing a Claim

1. Short-term disability must be exhausted first (or elimination period met)
2. HR coordinates the transition from STD to LTD
3. Insurance carrier contacts you for additional medical documentation
4. Continued certification from treating physician required every 90 days
5. Carrier may request independent medical examination

### During LTD

- Health insurance: Company continues to pay employer share for 12 months; COBRA available after
- Life insurance: Waiver of premium if approved by carrier
- 401(k): Contributions cease; account remains invested
- PTO: Does not accrue
- FMLA: Runs concurrently during first 12 weeks

### Returning to Work

- Gradual return-to-work programs supported
- Partial disability benefit available if returning part-time (proportional reduction)
- Rehabilitation services provided by the carrier if appropriate
- Contact HR to coordinate your return plan
Author: Talent Acquisition

Title: Adoption and surrogacy benefits — Engineering department
Content:
## Question

What adoption and surrogacy benefits are available to Engineering employees?

## Answer

The company supports all paths to parenthood with financial assistance and leave.

### Financial Assistance

| Benefit | Amount |
|---------|--------|
| Adoption Reimbursement | Up to $10,000 per child |
| Surrogacy Reimbursement | Up to $10,000 per child |
| Foster-to-Adopt Support | Up to $5,000 per child |
| Fertility Treatment | Up to $25,000 lifetime (under medical plan) |

### Eligible Adoption Expenses

- Agency and placement fees
- Legal and court fees
- Home study costs
- Travel expenses for domestic and international adoption
- Immigration and visa fees
- Required medical exams for the child
- Document translation fees

### How to Apply for Reimbursement

1. Notify HR that you are beginning the adoption/surrogacy process
2. Complete the Family Building Benefits Request on the HR Portal
3. After placement/birth, submit receipts and proof of finalization
4. Reimbursement processed within 30 days on your paycheck
5. Per-child maximum applies regardless of number of reimbursement submissions

### Leave Benefits

- **Adoption**: 12 weeks paid parental leave (same as birth parent leave)
- **Surrogacy (intended parent)**: 12 weeks paid parental leave
- **Foster Placement**: 6 weeks paid leave
- Leave begins on the date of placement or birth
- Additional unpaid FMLA leave available if needed

### Support Resources

- Adoption support group (employee-led, meets monthly)
- EAP counseling for adoption-related stress
- Bright Horizons backup childcare (20 days/year during transition)
- DCFSA: Up to $5,000/year pre-tax for eligible adoption expenses

### Important Notes

- Benefits available to all employees regardless of gender, marital status, or sexual orientation
- Must have been employed for 12+ months to be eligible for reimbursement
- Reimbursement is taxable income (company grosses up for taxes)
- Maximum 2 adoption/surrogacy reimbursements per employee
Author: HR Generalist

2. Complete the Family Building Benefits Request on the HR Portal
3. After placement/birth, submit receipts and proof of finalization
4. Reimbursement processed within 30 days on your paycheck
5. Per-child maximum applies regardless of number of reimbursement submissions

### Leave Benefits

- **Adoption**: 12 weeks paid parental leave (same as birth parent leave)
- **Surrogacy (intended parent)**: 12 weeks paid parental leave
- **Foster Placement**: 6 weeks paid leave
- Leave begins on the date of placement or birth
- Additional unpaid FMLA leave available if needed

### Support Resources

- Adoption support group (employee-led, meets monthly)
- EAP counseling for adoption-related stress
- Bright Horizons backup childcare (20 days/year during transition)
- DCFSA: Up to $5,000/year pre-tax for eligible adoption expenses

### Important Notes

- Benefits available to all employees regardless of gender, marital status, or sexual orientation
- Must have been employed for 12+ months to be eligible for reimbursement
- Reimbursement is taxable income (company grosses up for taxes)
- Maximum 2 adoption/surrogacy reimbursements per employee

Author: HR Compliance Team

Title: Employee assistance for student loans — Engineering department
Content:
## Question

What student loan assistance does the company offer to Engineering employees?

## Answer

The company helps employees manage education debt through multiple programs.

### Student Loan Repayment Assistance

- **Monthly Contribution**: $100/month toward your student loans (company-paid)
- **Eligibility**: Full-time employees after 6 months of service
- **Loan Types**: Federal and private student loans in the employee's name
- **Maximum**: $100/month, up to $10,000 lifetime
- **Tax Treatment**: Tax-free up to $5,250/year (per IRS Section 127)

### How to Enroll

1. Log in to the Benefits Portal > Student Loan Assistance
2. Enter your student loan servicer information
3. Provide loan account number and payment address
4. Company makes payments directly to your servicer each month
5. Payments begin the month following enrollment

### Refinancing Support

- Free consultation with a student loan advisor (through benefits partner)
- Group discount on refinancing rates through SoFi (0.25% rate reduction)
- No obligation to refinance
- Access at benefits.company.com > Student Loans > Refinancing

### Public Service Loan Forgiveness (PSLF) Guidance

- While this is a for-profit company (not PSLF-eligible), advisors can help if you have prior qualifying service
- Income-driven repayment plan optimization
- Loan consolidation guidance

### Additional Resources

| Resource | Details |
|----------|---------|
| Financial wellness counseling | 4 free sessions/year with certified financial planner |
| 529 college savings | Payroll deduction available to save for children's education |
| Tuition reimbursement | $5,250-$10,000/year for continuing education (see Tuition Reimbursement policy) |
| Emergency hardship fund | Grants available for financial emergencies |

### Leaving the Company

- Contributions stop when employment ends
- No repayment required for contributions already made
- No vesting period — contributions are yours immediately

Title: Inclement weather and office closure policy — Engineering department
Content:
## Question

What happens when severe weather or an emergency causes office closures affecting Engineering employees?

## Answer

The company prioritizes employee safety during inclement weather and emergencies.

### How Closures Are Communicated

1. **Decision**: Made by the COO by 5:30 AM (or as conditions develop)
2. **Notification channels** (all used simultaneously):
   - Company emergency notification system (text + email + phone)
   - Company website banner
   - Local news stations
   - Manager communication via team channels
   - Social media (company accounts)

### Closure Types

| Type | What It Means | Pay Impact |
|------|--------------|------------|
| Full Closure | Office closed all day | Full pay for all employees |
| Delayed Opening | Office opens late (e.g., 10 AM) | Full pay; arrive by announced time |
| Early Release | Office closes early | Full pay for scheduled shift |
| Remote Work Day | Office closed; work from home | Full pay; regular remote work |
| Essential Staff Only | Only designated roles report | Full pay for all; essential staff may receive premium |

### Essential Staff

- Designated by manager in advance (Security, IT Operations, Facilities)
- Essential staff who report during closures receive 1.5x pay
- If unable to safely commute, essential staff should notify their manager
- No one should risk their safety to report to work

### Remote Employees

- Expected to work as normal unless personally affected by the weather/emergency
- If personally affected (power outage, evacuation), notify your manager and use PTO or the emergency closure policy

### Pay During Closures

- **Exempt employees**: Paid in full for any partial-day closure
- **Non-exempt employees**: Paid for scheduled hours on closure days
- **Multi-day closure**: First 3 days paid; beyond that, PTO or emergency leave applies
- No employee will be required to use PTO for company-declared closures (first 3 days)

### Preparing for Inclement Weather

- Ensure your contact information is current in the emergency notification system
- Know your essential staff designation
- Take your laptop home if weather is expected overnight
- Charge your phone and have the company emergency number saved: 800-555-0999
Author: HR Compliance Team

- **Exempt employees**: Paid in full for any partial-day closure

- **Multi-day closure**: First 3 days paid; beyond that, PTO or emergency leave applies
- No employee will be required to use PTO for company-declared closures (first 3 days)

### Preparing for Inclement Weather

- Ensure your contact information is current in the emergency notification system
- Know your essential staff designation
- Take your laptop home if weather is expected overnight
- Charge your phone and have the company emergency number saved: 800-555-0999

Title: Unlimited PTO policy FAQ — Engineering department
Content:
## Question

How does the flexible time off policy work for exempt Engineering employees?

## Answer

Exempt employees at level L4 and above are eligible for the Flexible Time Off (FTO) policy instead of accrued PTO.

### How FTO Works

- No set number of vacation days — take time as needed
- No accrual, no cap, no carryover, no payout at separation
- Sick leave and other statutory leave are separate and still accrued
- All other leave types (FMLA, bereavement, jury duty, etc.) still apply

### Guidelines

| Guideline | Details |
|-----------|---------|
| Minimum | Take at least 15 days off per year (company expectation) |
| Maximum consecutive | Up to 3 weeks without VP approval; longer requires VP approval |
| Notice | 1 week notice for 1-2 days; 2 weeks for 3-5 days; 4 weeks for 1+ weeks |
| Busy periods | Coordinate with your team to ensure coverage |
| Manager approval | Required for all time off |

### Approval Process

1. Discuss with your manager
2. Submit request in the Time & Attendance system (select "Flexible Time Off")
3. Manager approves based on workload and team coverage
4. Block your calendar and set an out-of-office reply

### Manager Responsibilities

- Approve requests fairly and consistently across the team
- Ensure team members take at least the 15-day minimum
- If someone isn't taking enough time off, have a conversation
- Never deny time off as a punitive measure
- Model healthy time-off behavior (take time off yourself)

### Frequently Asked Questions

**Q: What if my manager never approves my time off?**
A: Escalate to HR. The policy is designed to be used, and managers are evaluated on supporting it.

**Q: Will taking time off affect my performance review?**
A: No. Taking appropriate time off is expected and will not negatively impact your review.

**Q: Can I take FTO during my first 90 days?**
A: Yes, with manager approval, but be mindful of the onboarding period.

**Q: What happens if I switch to a non-FTO-eligible role?**
A: You begin accruing PTO per the standard policy. No balance carries from FTO.

**Q: Is there a payout if I leave the company?**
A: No. FTO has no accrued balance and no payout upon separation.
Author: HR Benefits Team

Exempt employees at level L4 and above are eligible for the Flexible Time Off (FTO) policy instead of accrued PTO.

### How FTO Works

- No set number of vacation days — take time as needed
- No accrual, no cap, no carryover, no payout at separation
- Sick leave and other statutory leave are separate and still accrued
- All other leave types (FMLA, bereavement, jury duty, etc.) still apply

### Guidelines

| Guideline | Details |
|-----------|---------|
| Minimum | Take at least 15 days off per year (company expectation) |
| Maximum consecutive | Up to 3 weeks without VP approval; longer requires VP approval |
| Notice | 1 week notice for 1-2 days; 2 weeks for 3-5 days; 4 weeks for 1+ weeks |
| Busy periods | Coordinate with your team to ensure coverage |
| Manager approval | Required for all time off |

### Approval Process

1. Discuss with your manager
2. Submit request in the Time & Attendance system (select "Flexible Time Off")
3. Manager approves based on workload and team coverage
4. Block your calendar and set an out-of-office reply

### Manager Responsibilities

- Approve requests fairly and consistently across the team
- Ensure team members take at least the 15-day minimum
- If someone isn't taking enough time off, have a conversation
- Never deny time off as a punitive measure
- Model healthy time-off behavior (take time off yourself)

### Frequently Asked Questions

**Q: What if my manager never approves my time off?**
A: Escalate to HR. The policy is designed to be used, and managers are evaluated on supporting it.

**Q: Will taking time off affect my performance review?**
A: No. Taking appropriate time off is expected and will not negatively impact your review.

**Q: Can I take FTO during my first 90 days?**
A: Yes, with manager approval, but be mindful of the onboarding period.

**Q: What happens if I switch to a non-FTO-eligible role?**
A: You begin accruing PTO per the standard policy. No balance carries from FTO.

**Q: Is there a payout if I leave the company?**
A: No. FTO has no accrued balance and no payout upon separation.

Title: Manager's guide to onboarding a new hire — Engineering department
Content:
## Question

What should a Engineering manager do to prepare for and onboard a new team member?

## Answer

A great onboarding experience significantly impacts retention and time-to-productivity.

### Before Day 1 (2 weeks prior)

- [ ] Confirm start date and work location with HR
- [ ] Request IT equipment setup (laptop, monitors, accounts)
- [ ] Set up workspace (desk, chair, supplies, badge access)
- [ ] Prepare a first-week schedule
- [ ] Assign an onboarding buddy (peer on the team)
- [ ] Send a welcome email to the new hire with first-day logistics
- [ ] Notify the team about the new hire (name, role, start date, background)
- [ ] Prepare 30/60/90-day goals

### Day 1

- [ ] Greet the new hire personally (or virtually for remote hires)
- [ ] Tour the workspace and introduce to team members
- [ ] Have lunch together (company-paid on Day 1)
- [ ] Walk through the first-week schedule
- [ ] Review role expectations and 90-day goals
- [ ] Introduce the onboarding buddy
- [ ] Ensure all systems and access are working

### Week 1

- [ ] Daily check-ins (15-30 minutes)
- [ ] Introduce to key stakeholders and cross-functional partners
- [ ] Provide access to team documentation, wikis, and shared drives
- [ ] Walk through current projects and where the new hire will contribute
- [ ] Set up recurring 1:1 meeting (weekly recommended for first 90 days)

### Month 1

- [ ] Complete the 30-Day Check-in form
- [ ] Assign initial tasks or projects
- [ ] Ensure required training is on track
- [ ] Gather feedback from the new hire on their experience
- [ ] Introduce to broader organization (skip-level meeting, all-hands)

### Month 2-3

- [ ] Increase autonomy and project ownership
- [ ] 60-Day Check-in
- [ ] Provide substantive feedback on performance
- [ ] 90-Day Final Evaluation — confirm employment

### Common Mistakes to Avoid

- No plan for the first week (new hire sits idle)
- Information overload on Day 1
- Not making introductions
- Assuming the new hire will "figure things out"
- No regular check-ins during the first 90 days
- Forgetting to set up equipment before arrival

- [ ] Increase autonomy and project ownership
- [ ] 60-Day Check-in
- [ ] Provide substantive feedback on performance
- [ ] 90-Day Final Evaluation — confirm employment

### Common Mistakes to Avoid

- No plan for the first week (new hire sits idle)
- Information overload on Day 1
- Not making introductions
- Assuming the new hire will "figure things out"
- No regular check-ins during the first 90 days
- Forgetting to set up equipment before arrival
Author: HR Compliance Team

Title: Manager's guide to onboarding a new hire — Administration department
Content:
## Question

What should a Administration manager do to prepare for and onboard a new team member?

## Answer

A great onboarding experience significantly impacts retention and time-to-productivity.

### Before Day 1 (2 weeks prior)

- [ ] Confirm start date and work location with HR
- [ ] Request IT equipment setup (laptop, monitors, accounts)
- [ ] Set up workspace (desk, chair, supplies, badge access)
- [ ] Prepare a first-week schedule
- [ ] Assign an onboarding buddy (peer on the team)
- [ ] Send a welcome email to the new hire with first-day logistics
- [ ] Notify the team about the new hire (name, role, start date, background)
- [ ] Prepare 30/60/90-day goals

### Day 1

- [ ] Greet the new hire personally (or virtually for remote hires)
- [ ] Tour the workspace and introduce to team members
- [ ] Have lunch together (company-paid on Day 1)
- [ ] Walk through the first-week schedule
- [ ] Review role expectations and 90-day goals
- [ ] Introduce the onboarding buddy
- [ ] Ensure all systems and access are working

### Week 1

- [ ] Daily check-ins (15-30 minutes)
- [ ] Introduce to key stakeholders and cross-functional partners
- [ ] Provide access to team documentation, wikis, and shared drives
- [ ] Walk through current projects and where the new hire will contribute
- [ ] Set up recurring 1:1 meeting (weekly recommended for first 90 days)

### Month 1

- [ ] Complete the 30-Day Check-in form
- [ ] Assign initial tasks or projects
- [ ] Ensure required training is on track
- [ ] Gather feedback from the new hire on their experience
- [ ] Introduce to broader organization (skip-level meeting, all-hands)

### Month 2-3

- [ ] Increase autonomy and project ownership
- [ ] 60-Day Check-in
- [ ] Provide substantive feedback on performance
- [ ] 90-Day Final Evaluation — confirm employment

### Common Mistakes to Avoid

- No plan for the first week (new hire sits idle)
- Information overload on Day 1
- Not making introductions
- Assuming the new hire will "figure things out"
- No regular check-ins during the first 90 days
- Forgetting to set up equipment before arrival
Author: HR Generalist

Title: Involuntary termination process — Engineering department
Content:
## Question

What is the process for involuntary termination of a Engineering employee?

## Answer

Involuntary termination is a last resort after other corrective measures have been attempted (except in cases of gross misconduct).

### Types of Involuntary Termination

| Type | Description | Process |
|------|-------------|---------|
| Performance-Based | Failed PIP or sustained poor performance | Full PIP process required first |
| Misconduct | Violation of company policies | Investigation required |
| Gross Misconduct | Theft, violence, fraud, harassment | Immediate termination possible |
| Position Elimination | Role eliminated due to restructuring | Severance package offered |

### Pre-Termination Checklist (Manager + HR)

1. **Documentation review**: HR confirms documentation is complete
   - Performance reviews, PIP records, coaching notes, policy violations
2. **Legal review**: HR/Legal confirms compliance with employment law
   - No discriminatory factors
   - No retaliation concerns
   - Consistent treatment compared to similar situations
3. **Approval chain**: VP and HR VP approve the termination
4. **Logistics planned**:
   - Meeting time and location (private, end of day preferred)
   - IT prepares account deactivation (effective at meeting time)
   - Final paycheck calculated
   - Severance terms prepared (if applicable)
   - COBRA and benefits information ready

### The Termination Meeting

- **Attendees**: Manager, HR representative, and the employee
- **Duration**: 15-20 minutes
- **Script**:
  1. State the decision clearly and compassionately
  2. Briefly explain the reason (do not re-litigate)
  3. HR provides logistics: final pay, benefits, severance (if applicable), return of property
  4. Allow the employee to ask questions
  5. Provide the separation letter
  6. Coordinate departure (employee collects personal items, returns badge/equipment)

### Final Pay

- Paid on the day of termination (or next business day per state law)
- Includes: remaining salary, accrued PTO payout, prorated bonus (if applicable)
- Severance is separate (if offered)

### Severance (Position Elimination Only)

| Tenure | Severance |
|--------|-----------|
| Less than 1 year | 2 weeks |
| 1-5 years | 1 week per year of service |
| 5-10 years | 2 weeks per year of service |
| 10+ years | 3 weeks per year of service |

- Severance requires signing a separation agreement and release
- 21 days to review (40+ age: 45 days), 7-day revocation period
- Outplacement services provided for 3 months

### Post-Termination

- Notify the team (manager, with HR-approved messaging)
- Reassign work and responsibilities
- Update org chart and systems
- Conduct a brief debrief with HR

Title: How to handle underperforming employees — Engineering department
Content:
## Question

What steps should a Engineering manager take when an employee is underperforming?

## Answer

Address performance issues early with a structured, supportive approach.

### Step 1: Identify the Issue (Week 1)

- Define specifically what expectations are not being met
- Gather data: missed deadlines, quality issues, customer complaints, metrics
- Consider root causes:
  - Skill gap? (need training)
  - Will gap? (motivation, engagement)
  - External factors? (personal issues, health, workload)
  - Unclear expectations? (manager communication)
  - Resource gap? (tools, support, information)

### Step 2: Informal Coaching Conversation (Week 1-2)

1. Schedule a private 1:1 meeting
2. Share specific observations (not generalizations)
   - "I noticed the last 3 reports had data errors" vs. "Your work quality is poor"
3. Ask for the employee's perspective — listen before prescribing
4. Collaboratively agree on improvements and support needed
5. Set clear expectations with measurable outcomes and timeline
6. Document the conversation and send a follow-up summary email

### Step 3: Increased Monitoring (Weeks 2-6)

- Weekly check-ins focused on progress
- Provide real-time feedback (don't save it up)
- Recognize improvements immediately
- Offer resources: training, mentoring, adjusted workload
- Document each conversation

### Step 4: Formal Written Warning (If no improvement by Week 6)

1. Consult with HR before issuing
2. Deliver a formal written warning that includes:
   - Specific performance deficiencies
   - Prior coaching conversations (with dates)
   - Expected improvements and timeline (30 days)
   - Consequences if improvement is not sustained
3. Employee signs acknowledgment
4. Copy to HR for personnel file

### Step 5: Performance Improvement Plan (If still no improvement)

- See the PIP article for detailed guidance
- 30/60/90 day formal plan with weekly checkpoints
- HR involvement throughout
- Clear success criteria and consequences

### Step 6: Outcome

- **Improved**: Continue monitoring, acknowledge growth, close the process
- **Not improved**: Termination, demotion, or role change (consult HR)

### Key Principles

- Be timely — don't let issues fester
- Be specific — vague feedback is unhelpful
- Be compassionate — treat the employee with dignity
- Be consistent — apply the same standards to everyone
- Document everything — protect the employee and the company

Author: Talent Acquisition

Title: How to handle underperforming employees — Design department
Content:
## Question

What steps should a Design manager take when an employee is underperforming?

## Answer

Address performance issues early with a structured, supportive approach.

### Step 1: Identify the Issue (Week 1)

- Define specifically what expectations are not being met
- Gather data: missed deadlines, quality issues, customer complaints, metrics
- Consider root causes:
  - Skill gap? (need training)
  - Will gap? (motivation, engagement)
  - External factors? (personal issues, health, workload)
  - Unclear expectations? (manager communication)
  - Resource gap? (tools, support, information)

### Step 2: Informal Coaching Conversation (Week 1-2)

1. Schedule a private 1:1 meeting
2. Share specific observations (not generalizations)
   - "I noticed the last 3 reports had data errors" vs. "Your work quality is poor"
3. Ask for the employee's perspective — listen before prescribing
4. Collaboratively agree on improvements and support needed
5. Set clear expectations with measurable outcomes and timeline
6. Document the conversation and send a follow-up summary email

### Step 3: Increased Monitoring (Weeks 2-6)

- Weekly check-ins focused on progress
- Provide real-time feedback (don't save it up)
- Recognize improvements immediately
- Offer resources: training, mentoring, adjusted workload
- Document each conversation

### Step 4: Formal Written Warning (If no improvement by Week 6)

1. Consult with HR before issuing
2. Deliver a formal written warning that includes:
   - Specific performance deficiencies
   - Prior coaching conversations (with dates)
   - Expected improvements and timeline (30 days)
   - Consequences if improvement is not sustained
3. Employee signs acknowledgment
4. Copy to HR for personnel file

### Step 5: Performance Improvement Plan (If still no improvement)

- See the PIP article for detailed guidance
- 30/60/90 day formal plan with weekly checkpoints
- HR involvement throughout
- Clear success criteria and consequences

### Step 6: Outcome

- **Improved**: Continue monitoring, acknowledge growth, close the process
- **Not improved**: Termination, demotion, or role change (consult HR)

### Key Principles

- Be timely — don't let issues fester
- Be specific — vague feedback is unhelpful
- Be compassionate — treat the employee with dignity
- Be consistent — apply the same standards to everyone
- Document everything — protect the employee and the company

Title: Employee recognition programs — Engineering department
Content:
## Question

What employee recognition programs are available in Engineering?

## Answer

Recognition is a core part of our culture. Multiple programs exist for different types of acknowledgment.

### Peer-to-Peer Recognition: "Kudos"

- **What**: Any employee can recognize any other employee
- **How**: Use the "Give Kudos" button in the Performance Management Tool or Slack (#kudos channel)
- **Categories**: Living Our Values, Going Above and Beyond, Great Teamwork, Customer Hero, Innovation
- **Reward**: Badge on profile + 50 points in rewards program
- **Frequency**: Unlimited — recognize often!

### Manager Spot Awards

| Award Level | Amount | Approval |
|------------|--------|----------|
| Bronze | $100 gift card | Manager |
| Silver | $250 gift card | Director |
| Gold | $500 gift card | VP |

- For exceptional contributions, above-and-beyond effort, or significant impact
- Any manager can nominate; approval per table above
- Processed through HR; gift card delivered within 5 business days

### Quarterly Excellence Awards

- Each department selects one recipient per quarter
- **Criteria**: Outstanding results, exemplifying company values, significant contribution
- **Reward**: $1,000 bonus + featured in company newsletter + reserved parking for the quarter
- **Nomination**: Peers and managers submit nominations via the HR Portal
- **Selection**: Department leadership committee

### Annual Awards

| Award | Description | Prize |
|-------|-------------|-------|
| Employee of the Year | Overall excellence across all dimensions | $5,000 + extra PTO week |
| Innovation Award | Best new idea or process improvement | $3,000 + conference trip |
| Customer Champion | Outstanding customer service | $2,000 + trophy |
| Culture Ambassador | Best embodiment of company values | $2,000 + trophy |
| Rising Star | Outstanding new employee (< 2 years) | $1,500 + development budget |

### Service Milestone Awards

| Years | Recognition |
|-------|------------|
| 5 years | Personalized gift ($250 value) + company announcement |
| 10 years | Gift ($500 value) + celebration lunch + extra PTO day |
| 15 years | Gift ($750 value) + celebration dinner + 2 extra PTO days |
| 20 years | Gift ($1,000 value) + celebration event + 3 extra PTO days |
| 25+ years | Crystal award + $2,000 gift + celebration event + 5 extra PTO days |

### Rewards Points Program

- Earn points from Kudos, wellness activities, learning completions, and referrals
- Redeem at rewards.company.com for gift cards, merchandise, experiences, or charitable donations
- Points expire 12 months after earning
Author: HR Operations

Title: How to request a flexible work schedule — Engineering department
Content:
## Question

Can Engineering employees request a flexible or alternative work schedule?

## Answer

The company supports flexible scheduling to promote work-life balance and productivity.

### Schedule Options

| Option | Description | Example |
|--------|-------------|---------|
| Flex Time | Adjust daily start/end times | 7 AM - 3:30 PM instead of 9-5:30 |
| Compressed Week | Longer days, fewer days | 4 x 10-hour days (Mon-Thu) |
| 9/80 Schedule | Every other Friday off | 9-hour days with alternating Fridays off |
| Part-Time | Reduced hours | 20-30 hours/week |
| Job Share | Two employees share one role | Each works 20 hours/week |

### Eligibility

- Full-time employees who have completed 90 days
- Satisfactory performance rating (3+)
- Role is suitable for the requested schedule
- Manager approval required

### How to Request

1. Discuss the idea with your manager informally first
2. Submit a Flexible Schedule Request via the HR Portal
3. Include:
   - Proposed schedule with specific hours/days
   - How you will ensure coverage and collaboration
   - Impact on team, clients, and stakeholders
   - Proposed trial period (typically 90 days)
4. Manager reviews with HR input and responds within 10 business days

### Trial Period

- All flexible schedules start with a 90-day trial
- Weekly check-ins during the trial to assess impact
- At the end of the trial: continue, modify, or revert to standard schedule
- Either party can end the arrangement with 2 weeks' notice

### Guidelines

- **Core Hours**: All employees must be available 10 AM - 2 PM for meetings and collaboration (unless compressed week day off)
- **Communication**: Update your calendar and set status to show your schedule
- **Overtime**: Compressed schedules do not change overtime eligibility (non-exempt employees still earn OT over 40 hrs/week)
- **Benefits**: Part-time (20+ hours) retains full benefits; under 20 hours loses benefits eligibility

### Compensation Impact

| Schedule | Compensation |
|----------|-------------|
| Flex Time | No change |
| Compressed Week | No change |
| 9/80 | No change |
| Part-Time | Prorated salary and PTO |
| Job Share | Prorated salary and PTO |
Author: HR Benefits Team

Author: Learning & Development

Title: How to request a flexible work schedule — Finance department
Content:
## Question

Can Finance employees request a flexible or alternative work schedule?

## Answer

The company supports flexible scheduling to promote work-life balance and productivity.

### Schedule Options

| Option | Description | Example |
|--------|-------------|---------|
| Flex Time | Adjust daily start/end times | 7 AM - 3:30 PM instead of 9-5:30 |
| Compressed Week | Longer days, fewer days | 4 x 10-hour days (Mon-Thu) |
| 9/80 Schedule | Every other Friday off | 9-hour days with alternating Fridays off |
| Part-Time | Reduced hours | 20-30 hours/week |
| Job Share | Two employees share one role | Each works 20 hours/week |

### Eligibility

- Full-time employees who have completed 90 days
- Satisfactory performance rating (3+)
- Role is suitable for the requested schedule
- Manager approval required

### How to Request

1. Discuss the idea with your manager informally first
2. Submit a Flexible Schedule Request via the HR Portal
3. Include:
   - Proposed schedule with specific hours/days
   - How you will ensure coverage and collaboration
   - Impact on team, clients, and stakeholders
   - Proposed trial period (typically 90 days)
4. Manager reviews with HR input and responds within 10 business days

### Trial Period

- All flexible schedules start with a 90-day trial
- Weekly check-ins during the trial to assess impact
- At the end of the trial: continue, modify, or revert to standard schedule
- Either party can end the arrangement with 2 weeks' notice

### Guidelines

- **Core Hours**: All employees must be available 10 AM - 2 PM for meetings and collaboration (unless compressed week day off)
- **Communication**: Update your calendar and set status to show your schedule
- **Overtime**: Compressed schedules do not change overtime eligibility (non-exempt employees still earn OT over 40 hrs/week)
- **Benefits**: Part-time (20+ hours) retains full benefits; under 20 hours loses benefits eligibility

### Compensation Impact

| Schedule | Compensation |
|----------|-------------|
| Flex Time | No change |
| Compressed Week | No change |
| 9/80 | No change |
| Part-Time | Prorated salary and PTO |
| Job Share | Prorated salary and PTO |

Author: Employee Relations Team

Title: Whistleblower protection policy — Engineering department
Content:
## Question

What protections are available for Engineering employees who report wrongdoing?

## Answer

The company encourages reporting of suspected illegal, unethical, or improper conduct and provides strong protections for those who report in good faith.

### What to Report

- Financial fraud or accounting irregularities
- Bribery, kickbacks, or corruption
- Securities violations or insider trading
- Environmental violations
- Safety hazards or OSHA violations
- Discrimination, harassment, or retaliation
- Theft, embezzlement, or misuse of company assets
- Violation of any law, regulation, or company policy
- Conflicts of interest

### How to Report

| Channel | When to Use |
|---------|------------|
| Direct Manager | First line for most concerns |
| HR | If manager is involved or you prefer HR |
| Ethics Hotline: 800-555-0789 | Anonymous reporting available |
| ethics.company.com | Online anonymous reporting |
| General Counsel | Legal or compliance matters |
| Audit Committee | Financial fraud or executive misconduct |
| External agencies (SEC, OSHA, etc.) | Employees always have this right |

### Protections

**Anti-Retaliation Guarantee**
The company strictly prohibits retaliation against anyone who:
- Makes a good-faith report of suspected wrongdoing
- Participates in an investigation
- Refuses to participate in conduct they believe is illegal

**Protected from:**
- Termination, demotion, or suspension
- Threats, harassment, or intimidation
- Reduced pay, hours, or responsibilities
- Negative performance reviews motivated by the report
- Transfer or reassignment as punishment
- Any other adverse employment action

### Investigation Process

1. Report received and logged (within 24 hours)
2. Preliminary assessment by Compliance/Legal
3. Investigation conducted by trained investigators
4. Findings reported to appropriate leadership
5. Corrective action taken if warranted
6. Reporter notified of resolution (within confidentiality limits)

### If You Experience Retaliation

Report it immediately through any channel above. Retaliation is a separate, serious violation that will result in disciplinary action against the retaliator, up to and including termination.

### Legal Protections

- Sarbanes-Oxley Act (financial fraud reporting)
- Dodd-Frank Act (SEC whistleblower program — financial rewards possible)
- State whistleblower statutes
- The company's protections exceed legal minimums

Title: Travel policy and per diem rates — Engineering department
Content:
## Question

What is the business travel policy for Engineering employees?

## Answer

All business travel must be pre-approved and follow these guidelines.

### Approval Requirements

| Trip Cost | Approval Needed |
|-----------|----------------|
| Under $1,000 | Manager |
| $1,000 - $5,000 | Director |
| $5,000 - $15,000 | VP |
| Over $15,000 | SVP/C-level |

### Booking Travel

- Book through the company travel portal: travel.company.com
- Book flights and hotels at least 14 days in advance when possible
- For last-minute travel (< 7 days), additional approval from Director required

### Airfare

| Flight Duration | Class |
|----------------|-------|
| Under 4 hours domestic | Economy |
| 4-6 hours | Economy (Premium Economy with VP approval) |
| Over 6 hours / International | Premium Economy (Business Class for VP+) |

- Lowest logical fare within 2 hours of preferred time
- Personal travel combined with business: Only business portion reimbursed
- Frequent flyer miles: Employee retains for personal use

### Hotels

| City Tier | Nightly Rate Cap |
|-----------|-----------------|
| Tier 1 (NYC, SF, DC, London) | $350/night |
| Tier 2 (Chicago, Boston, LA, Seattle) | $275/night |
| Tier 3 (All other cities) | $200/night |
| International | Per country rate (see travel portal) |

### Meals (Per Diem)

| Meal | Daily Allowance |
|------|----------------|
| Breakfast | $20 |
| Lunch | $25 |
| Dinner | $40 |
| **Total Daily** | **$85** |

- Per diem covers meals only (no alcohol)
- Client entertainment requires pre-approval and itemized receipt
- Room service counts toward meal per diem

### Ground Transportation

- Rideshare (Uber/Lyft) preferred for short trips
- Rental car: Standard/intermediate class; always purchase insurance
- Personal vehicle: $0.70/mile (2026 IRS rate)
- Parking and tolls: Reimbursable with receipts

### Expense Submission

- Submit all expenses within 30 days of travel completion
- Receipts required for all expenses over $25
- Use the expense management system: expenses.company.com
- Reimbursement on next payroll cycle after manager approval

| Trip Cost | Approval Needed |
|-----------|----------------|
| Under $1,000 | Manager |
| $1,000 - $5,000 | Director |
| $5,000 - $15,000 | VP |
| Over $15,000 | SVP/C-level |

### Booking Travel

- Book through the company travel portal: travel.company.com
- Book flights and hotels at least 14 days in advance when possible
- For last-minute travel (< 7 days), additional approval from Director required

### Airfare

| Flight Duration | Class |
|----------------|-------|
| Under 4 hours domestic | Economy |
| 4-6 hours | Economy (Premium Economy with VP approval) |
| Over 6 hours / International | Premium Economy (Business Class for VP+) |

- Lowest logical fare within 2 hours of preferred time
- Personal travel combined with business: Only business portion reimbursed
- Frequent flyer miles: Employee retains for personal use

### Hotels

| City Tier | Nightly Rate Cap |
|-----------|-----------------|
| Tier 1 (NYC, SF, DC, London) | $350/night |
| Tier 2 (Chicago, Boston, LA, Seattle) | $275/night |
| Tier 3 (All other cities) | $200/night |
| International | Per country rate (see travel portal) |

### Meals (Per Diem)

| Meal | Daily Allowance |
|------|----------------|
| Breakfast | $20 |
| Lunch | $25 |
| Dinner | $40 |
| **Total Daily** | **$85** |

- Per diem covers meals only (no alcohol)
- Client entertainment requires pre-approval and itemized receipt
- Room service counts toward meal per diem

### Ground Transportation

- Rideshare (Uber/Lyft) preferred for short trips
- Rental car: Standard/intermediate class; always purchase insurance
- Personal vehicle: $0.70/mile (2026 IRS rate)
- Parking and tolls: Reimbursable with receipts

### Expense Submission

- Submit all expenses within 30 days of travel completion
- Receipts required for all expenses over $25
- Use the expense management system: expenses.company.com
- Reimbursement on next payroll cycle after manager approval

Author: Learning & Development

Title: Garnishment and wage deduction policy — Engineering department
Content:
## Question

What happens when a Engineering employee has a wage garnishment or court-ordered deduction?

## Answer

The company is legally required to comply with all valid garnishment orders.

### Types of Garnishments

| Type | Maximum Deduction | Priority |
|------|------------------|----------|
| Child Support | Up to 50-65% of disposable earnings | First priority |
| Federal Tax Levy (IRS) | Per IRS tables (varies) | Second priority |
| State Tax Levy | Per state law | Third priority |
| Student Loan (federal) | Up to 15% of disposable earnings | Fourth priority |
| Creditor Garnishment | Up to 25% of disposable earnings | Fifth priority |
| Bankruptcy | Per court order | Varies |

### What Happens When We Receive an Order

1. Payroll receives the garnishment order
2. Legal validity is confirmed
3. Employee receives a copy of the order (unless law prohibits notification)
4. Deductions begin on the next full pay period
5. Payments are remitted directly to the garnishing agency

### Employee Rights

- **No retaliation**: Federal law prohibits termination for a single garnishment
- **Right to contest**: You may dispute the garnishment with the issuing court
- **Exemptions**: Some income may be exempt from garnishment (consult an attorney)
- **Notification**: You will be notified whenever possible before deductions begin
- **Multiple garnishments**: Priority rules apply; there are maximum total deduction limits

### If You Receive a Garnishment Notice

1. Review the order carefully
2. Contact Payroll with any questions: payroll@company.com (all communications are confidential)
3. If you wish to contest, consult an attorney (EAP provides a free 30-minute legal consultation)
4. Provide any exemption documentation to Payroll
5. The company must comply with valid orders regardless of employee dispute

### Voluntary Deductions vs. Garnishments

- Voluntary deductions (401k, benefits, etc.) are generally taken before garnishment calculations
- Garnishment is calculated on "disposable earnings" (gross pay minus legally required deductions)

### Confidentiality

- Garnishment information is confidential between Payroll, Legal, and the employee
- Your manager is not informed about garnishments
- Information is stored in a separate, restricted file

| Federal Tax Levy (IRS) | Per IRS tables (varies) | Second priority |
| State Tax Levy | Per state law | Third priority |
| Student Loan (federal) | Up to 15% of disposable earnings | Fourth priority |
| Creditor Garnishment | Up to 25% of disposable earnings | Fifth priority |
| Bankruptcy | Per court order | Varies |

### What Happens When We Receive an Order

1. Payroll receives the garnishment order
2. Legal validity is confirmed
3. Employee receives a copy of the order (unless law prohibits notification)
4. Deductions begin on the next full pay period
5. Payments are remitted directly to the garnishing agency

### Employee Rights

- **No retaliation**: Federal law prohibits termination for a single garnishment
- **Right to contest**: You may dispute the garnishment with the issuing court
- **Exemptions**: Some income may be exempt from garnishment (consult an attorney)
- **Notification**: You will be notified whenever possible before deductions begin
- **Multiple garnishments**: Priority rules apply; there are maximum total deduction limits

### If You Receive a Garnishment Notice

1. Review the order carefully
2. Contact Payroll with any questions: payroll@company.com (all communications are confidential)
3. If you wish to contest, consult an attorney (EAP provides a free 30-minute legal consultation)
4. Provide any exemption documentation to Payroll
5. The company must comply with valid orders regardless of employee dispute

### Voluntary Deductions vs. Garnishments

- Voluntary deductions (401k, benefits, etc.) are generally taken before garnishment calculations
- Garnishment is calculated on "disposable earnings" (gross pay minus legally required deductions)

### Confidentiality

- Garnishment information is confidential between Payroll, Legal, and the employee
- Your manager is not informed about garnishments
- Information is stored in a separate, restricted file

Author: Talent Acquisition

Title: Succession planning process — Engineering department
Content:
## Question

How does succession planning work for key roles in Engineering?

## Answer

Succession planning ensures business continuity by identifying and developing future leaders for critical positions.

### What Is Succession Planning

- Proactive identification of employees who can fill key roles if they become vacant
- Development of those employees to prepare them for future roles
- Ensures the organization is not overly dependent on any single person

### Which Roles Have Succession Plans

- All VP and above positions (required)
- All Director positions (required)
- Critical individual contributor roles identified by department leadership
- "Key person risk" roles identified during business continuity planning

### The Process

**Annual Review (September - October)**

1. **Identify Critical Roles**: Department heads identify roles essential to operations
2. **Assess Current Bench**: For each role, identify potential successors:
   - **Ready Now**: Could assume the role within 0-6 months
   - **Ready in 1-2 Years**: Needs targeted development
   - **Ready in 3+ Years**: High potential, needs significant development
3. **Assess Readiness**: Evaluate candidates against role requirements
   - Technical/functional skills
   - Leadership competencies
   - Cultural fit and values alignment
   - Career aspiration (does the person want the role?)
4. **Development Plans**: Create individual development plans for successors
5. **Review with HR**: Department plans reviewed with HR for consistency and cross-functional opportunities

### Development Actions for Successors

| Action | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| Stretch assignments | Build new skills through challenging projects |
| Cross-functional rotation | Broaden perspective and knowledge |
| Executive exposure | Shadow senior leaders, present to executives |
| External training | Leadership programs, conferences, certifications |
| Mentoring/coaching | Pair with a senior leader or executive coach |
| Acting roles | Serve as interim leader during absences |

### Confidentiality

- Succession plans are confidential to HR and leadership
- Being "on the succession plan" is not a guarantee of promotion
- Successors are generally not told they are on the plan (to manage expectations)
- Development conversations happen naturally through career discussions

### Employee's Role

- Express career aspirations to your manager
- Seek stretch assignments and cross-functional experience
- Develop leadership skills proactively
- Be open to feedback and development opportunities
- The best way to be a succession candidate is to excel in your current role and show growth potential
Author: Learning & Development

Title: Succession planning process — Logistics department
Content:
## Question

How does succession planning work for key roles in Logistics?

## Answer

Succession planning ensures business continuity by identifying and developing future leaders for critical positions.

### What Is Succession Planning

- Proactive identification of employees who can fill key roles if they become vacant
- Development of those employees to prepare them for future roles
- Ensures the organization is not overly dependent on any single person

### Which Roles Have Succession Plans

- All VP and above positions (required)
- All Director positions (required)
- Critical individual contributor roles identified by department leadership
- "Key person risk" roles identified during business continuity planning

### The Process

**Annual Review (September - October)**

1. **Identify Critical Roles**: Department heads identify roles essential to operations
2. **Assess Current Bench**: For each role, identify potential successors:
   - **Ready Now**: Could assume the role within 0-6 months
   - **Ready in 1-2 Years**: Needs targeted development
   - **Ready in 3+ Years**: High potential, needs significant development
3. **Assess Readiness**: Evaluate candidates against role requirements
   - Technical/functional skills
   - Leadership competencies
   - Cultural fit and values alignment
   - Career aspiration (does the person want the role?)
4. **Development Plans**: Create individual development plans for successors
5. **Review with HR**: Department plans reviewed with HR for consistency and cross-functional opportunities

### Development Actions for Successors

| Action | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| Stretch assignments | Build new skills through challenging projects |
| Cross-functional rotation | Broaden perspective and knowledge |
| Executive exposure | Shadow senior leaders, present to executives |
| External training | Leadership programs, conferences, certifications |
| Mentoring/coaching | Pair with a senior leader or executive coach |
| Acting roles | Serve as interim leader during absences |

### Confidentiality

- Succession plans are confidential to HR and leadership
- Being "on the succession plan" is not a guarantee of promotion
- Successors are generally not told they are on the plan (to manage expectations)
- Development conversations happen naturally through career discussions

### Employee's Role

- Express career aspirations to your manager
- Seek stretch assignments and cross-functional experience
- Develop leadership skills proactively
- Be open to feedback and development opportunities
- The best way to be a succession candidate is to excel in your current role and show growth potential
Author: HR Generalist

Title: Pandemic and infectious disease preparedness — Engineering department
Content:
## Question

What is the company's pandemic and infectious disease preparedness plan for Engineering employees?

## Answer

The company maintains a comprehensive preparedness plan to protect employees during infectious disease outbreaks.

### Prevention Measures (Ongoing)

- Enhanced cleaning and disinfection of high-touch surfaces
- Hand sanitizer stations at all entrances and common areas
- HVAC systems with enhanced filtration (MERV-13 filters)
- Touchless fixtures in restrooms
- Annual flu shot clinics (free for all employees)

### Response Levels

| Level | Trigger | Actions |
|-------|---------|---------|
| Level 1: Monitor | WHO/CDC advisory | Communications to employees, enhanced hygiene |
| Level 2: Prepare | Local cases reported | Social distancing, masks available, travel restricted |
| Level 3: Respond | Community spread confirmed | Remote work activated, office capacity limited, visitors restricted |
| Level 4: Full Response | Widespread community impact | Office closed, full remote work, events cancelled |

### Employee Responsibilities

- Stay home if you are sick (no exceptions)
- Report positive test results for reportable diseases to HR (confidential)
- Follow all posted health protocols (masking, distancing, screening)
- Get recommended vaccinations (encouraged but not required except where legally mandated)
- Keep emergency contact and medical information updated

### If You Test Positive

1. Notify your manager and HR immediately (confidential)
2. Do not come to the office until cleared
3. Follow isolation guidelines per CDC recommendations
4. HR conducts confidential contact tracing (if applicable)
5. Return-to-work requires symptom-free for 24 hours (or per CDC guidance)

### Compensation During Illness

- Use sick leave for the first 10 days
- Emergency Paid Sick Leave may be activated during declared emergencies
- Short-term disability available for extended illness
- No employee will lose their job due to a pandemic-related illness

### Remote Work During Outbreaks

- All employees should be prepared to work remotely at any time
- Take your laptop home daily during elevated response levels
- Test VPN and video conferencing monthly
- Keep a current home workspace setup

### Travel During Outbreaks

- All non-essential business travel suspended at Level 2+
- Essential travel requires VP and HR approval
- Personal travel may trigger quarantine upon return (per current guidelines)
- Monitor travel advisories at travel.company.com

### Resources

- Employee Health Line: ext. 5555
- CDC Information: cdc.gov
- EAP (stress, anxiety): 800-555-0123
Author: Talent Acquisition

Author: HR Benefits Team

Title: Pandemic and infectious disease preparedness — Executive department
Content:
## Question

What is the company's pandemic and infectious disease preparedness plan for Executive employees?

## Answer

The company maintains a comprehensive preparedness plan to protect employees during infectious disease outbreaks.

### Prevention Measures (Ongoing)

- Enhanced cleaning and disinfection of high-touch surfaces
- Hand sanitizer stations at all entrances and common areas
- HVAC systems with enhanced filtration (MERV-13 filters)
- Touchless fixtures in restrooms
- Annual flu shot clinics (free for all employees)

### Response Levels

| Level | Trigger | Actions |
|-------|---------|---------|
| Level 1: Monitor | WHO/CDC advisory | Communications to employees, enhanced hygiene |
| Level 2: Prepare | Local cases reported | Social distancing, masks available, travel restricted |
| Level 3: Respond | Community spread confirmed | Remote work activated, office capacity limited, visitors restricted |
| Level 4: Full Response | Widespread community impact | Office closed, full remote work, events cancelled |

### Employee Responsibilities

- Stay home if you are sick (no exceptions)
- Report positive test results for reportable diseases to HR (confidential)
- Follow all posted health protocols (masking, distancing, screening)
- Get recommended vaccinations (encouraged but not required except where legally mandated)
- Keep emergency contact and medical information updated

### If You Test Positive

1. Notify your manager and HR immediately (confidential)
2. Do not come to the office until cleared
3. Follow isolation guidelines per CDC recommendations
4. HR conducts confidential contact tracing (if applicable)
5. Return-to-work requires symptom-free for 24 hours (or per CDC guidance)

### Compensation During Illness

- Use sick leave for the first 10 days
- Emergency Paid Sick Leave may be activated during declared emergencies
- Short-term disability available for extended illness
- No employee will lose their job due to a pandemic-related illness

### Remote Work During Outbreaks

- All employees should be prepared to work remotely at any time
- Take your laptop home daily during elevated response levels
- Test VPN and video conferencing monthly
- Keep a current home workspace setup

### Travel During Outbreaks

- All non-essential business travel suspended at Level 2+
- Essential travel requires VP and HR approval
- Personal travel may trigger quarantine upon return (per current guidelines)
- Monitor travel advisories at travel.company.com

### Resources

- Employee Health Line: ext. 5555
- CDC Information: cdc.gov
- EAP (stress, anxiety): 800-555-0123

Author: Learning & Development

Title: Contractor to employee conversion — Engineering department
Content:
## Question

How does the contractor-to-employee conversion process work in Engineering?

## Answer

Converting a contractor to a full-time employee (FTE) involves several steps to ensure compliance and proper onboarding.

### When to Consider Conversion

- Contractor has been engaged for 12+ months
- Role is ongoing with no defined end date
- Work is core to the business (not a temporary project)
- Contractor meets IRS criteria for employee classification
- Budget exists for the FTE position

### Process

**Step 1: Manager Initiates Request**
1. Submit a Contractor Conversion Request via the HR Portal
2. Include business justification and proposed role details
3. Obtain Finance approval for headcount and budget

**Step 2: HR and Legal Review**
- HR reviews for proper worker classification compliance
- Legal reviews existing contract terms (non-compete, IP assignment, notice period)
- Compensation analysis to determine appropriate salary and level

**Step 3: Position Created**
- HR creates the job requisition
- Position may be posted internally/externally per policy (some conversions are direct)
- Direct conversion: Manager recommends, VP approves

**Step 4: Offer Extended**
- Standard offer letter issued with FTE terms
- Benefits eligibility from the first day of employment (not a new hire waiting period if converting from long-term contract)
- Background check required (even if done when contracting)

**Step 5: Contract Termination**
- Work with Procurement to properly end the contractor engagement
- Observe required notice period in the contract
- Gap between contract end and employment start: usually 0 days, but up to 2 weeks may be required

**Step 6: Onboarding**
- Standard new hire onboarding process
- I-9 verification required (even if previously completed as contractor)
- Benefits enrollment within 30 days
- Some company policies have different applicability dates for converted contractors

### Compensation Considerations

- FTE salary may be lower than contractor hourly rate (because benefits add 25-35% to total compensation)
- Conversion calculator available from HR to show total compensation comparison
- Signing bonus may be offered to bridge the transition

### Common Questions

**Q: Does tenure as a contractor count toward company benefits?**
A: Generally no. Benefits tenure starts from FTE hire date. Exception: Some companies credit contractor time for PTO accrual and sabbatical eligibility (check with HR).

**Q: Can a contractor decline conversion and continue contracting?**
A: Potentially, but if the role requires employee classification per IRS rules, continued contracting may not be an option.

**Q: Is there a limit on how long a contractor can work before conversion?**
A: The company's policy is 18 months maximum for most contractor engagements. Extensions require VP and Legal approval.

- HR reviews for proper worker classification compliance
- Legal reviews existing contract terms (non-compete, IP assignment, notice period)
- Compensation analysis to determine appropriate salary and level

**Step 3: Position Created**
- HR creates the job requisition
- Position may be posted internally/externally per policy (some conversions are direct)
- Direct conversion: Manager recommends, VP approves

**Step 4: Offer Extended**
- Standard offer letter issued with FTE terms
- Benefits eligibility from the first day of employment (not a new hire waiting period if converting from long-term contract)
- Background check required (even if done when contracting)

**Step 5: Contract Termination**
- Work with Procurement to properly end the contractor engagement
- Observe required notice period in the contract
- Gap between contract end and employment start: usually 0 days, but up to 2 weeks may be required

**Step 6: Onboarding**
- Standard new hire onboarding process
- I-9 verification required (even if previously completed as contractor)
- Benefits enrollment within 30 days
- Some company policies have different applicability dates for converted contractors

### Compensation Considerations

- FTE salary may be lower than contractor hourly rate (because benefits add 25-35% to total compensation)
- Conversion calculator available from HR to show total compensation comparison
- Signing bonus may be offered to bridge the transition

### Common Questions

**Q: Does tenure as a contractor count toward company benefits?**
A: Generally no. Benefits tenure starts from FTE hire date. Exception: Some companies credit contractor time for PTO accrual and sabbatical eligibility (check with HR).

**Q: Can a contractor decline conversion and continue contracting?**
A: Potentially, but if the role requires employee classification per IRS rules, continued contracting may not be an option.

**Q: Is there a limit on how long a contractor can work before conversion?**
A: The company's policy is 18 months maximum for most contractor engagements. Extensions require VP and Legal approval.

Author: HR Benefits Team

Title: Diversity and inclusion in hiring — Engineering department
Content:
## Question

What diversity and inclusion practices are followed in Engineering hiring?

## Answer

The company is committed to building a diverse workforce through equitable and inclusive hiring practices.

### Inclusive Job Postings

- All job descriptions reviewed for inclusive language (gender-neutral, no unnecessary requirements)
- Use the company's Inclusive Language Guide (available on HR Portal)
- Focus on skills and outcomes, not years of experience (where possible)
- Include the EEO statement and accommodation language
- Post on diverse job boards in addition to standard channels

### Diverse Candidate Slates

- **Rooney Rule**: At least one candidate from an underrepresented group must be interviewed for every role at Manager level and above
- Recruiters proactively source from diverse talent pools
- Partnerships with diversity-focused organizations and universities
- Employee resource groups assist with sourcing and referrals

### Structured Interviews

- All interviewers use the same question set and scoring rubric
- Behavioral and situational questions (not "culture fit" questions)
- Interviewers trained on unconscious bias (annual training required)
- Interview panels should be diverse in gender, race, and functional background

### Reducing Bias

| Practice | How It Helps |
|----------|-------------|
| Structured interviews | Consistent evaluation criteria |
| Blind resume review | Remove names, photos, school names in initial screen |
| Diverse interview panels | Multiple perspectives reduce individual bias |
| Bias training | Awareness of common biases and how to mitigate |
| Calibration sessions | Ensure consistency across interviewers |
| Data tracking | Monitor diversity metrics at each funnel stage |

### Accommodations for Candidates

- Provide interview accommodations upon request (sign language interpreter, extra time, alternative formats)
- Virtual interview option for all candidates
- Accessible interview locations (ADA compliant)
- Flexible scheduling for candidates with constraints

### Metrics and Accountability

- Diversity hiring data reported quarterly to leadership
- Each department has representation goals (not quotas)
- Hiring manager training on inclusive hiring is mandatory
- Recruiter incentives aligned with diverse slate requirements
- Annual audit of hiring processes for equity

### Employee Resource Groups (ERGs)

ERGs often participate in recruiting activities:
- Attend career fairs representing the company
- Review job descriptions for inclusive language
- Participate in interview panels
- Mentor new hires from underrepresented groups

### Contact

- DEI Team: dei@company.com
- Talent Acquisition: recruiting@company.com
- Accommodations: accommodations@company.com

Title: Diversity and inclusion in hiring — Legal department
Content:
## Question

What diversity and inclusion practices are followed in Legal hiring?

## Answer

The company is committed to building a diverse workforce through equitable and inclusive hiring practices.

### Inclusive Job Postings

- All job descriptions reviewed for inclusive language (gender-neutral, no unnecessary requirements)
- Use the company's Inclusive Language Guide (available on HR Portal)
- Focus on skills and outcomes, not years of experience (where possible)
- Include the EEO statement and accommodation language
- Post on diverse job boards in addition to standard channels

### Diverse Candidate Slates

- **Rooney Rule**: At least one candidate from an underrepresented group must be interviewed for every role at Manager level and above
- Recruiters proactively source from diverse talent pools
- Partnerships with diversity-focused organizations and universities
- Employee resource groups assist with sourcing and referrals

### Structured Interviews

- All interviewers use the same question set and scoring rubric
- Behavioral and situational questions (not "culture fit" questions)
- Interviewers trained on unconscious bias (annual training required)
- Interview panels should be diverse in gender, race, and functional background

### Reducing Bias

| Practice | How It Helps |
|----------|-------------|
| Structured interviews | Consistent evaluation criteria |
| Blind resume review | Remove names, photos, school names in initial screen |
| Diverse interview panels | Multiple perspectives reduce individual bias |
| Bias training | Awareness of common biases and how to mitigate |
| Calibration sessions | Ensure consistency across interviewers |
| Data tracking | Monitor diversity metrics at each funnel stage |

### Accommodations for Candidates

- Provide interview accommodations upon request (sign language interpreter, extra time, alternative formats)
- Virtual interview option for all candidates
- Accessible interview locations (ADA compliant)
- Flexible scheduling for candidates with constraints

### Metrics and Accountability

- Diversity hiring data reported quarterly to leadership
- Each department has representation goals (not quotas)
- Hiring manager training on inclusive hiring is mandatory
- Recruiter incentives aligned with diverse slate requirements
- Annual audit of hiring processes for equity
